perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Party A and Party B unanimously agree that they will allocate the proceeds generated from cooperation in accordance with thefollowing provisions: Party B shall have the right to charge, on a quarterly basis, the service fee ("Service Fee") from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services, or designate another person to charge Service Fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. The total sum of Service Fee shall be equal to the amount of profits gained by Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. Party B shall have the right to adjust the amount of Service Fee at its own discretion, without the prior consent of Party A or its subsidiaries. Party A shall cause its subsidiaries to pay the Service Fee in respect of Party B's Services provided to such subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Party A and Party B unanimously agree that they will allocate the proceeds generated from cooperation in accordance with thefollowing provisions: Party B shall have the right to charge, on a quarterly basis, a fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services, or designate another person to charge a fee from Party A or its subsidiaries who have accepted Party B's Services. Party A shall cause its subsidiaries to pay the fee in respect of Party B's Services provided to such subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause that specifies the fee as "Service Fee" and that the total sum of the Service Fee shall be equal to the amount of profits gained by Party A, while also removing the section where Party B has the right to adjust the amount of Service Fee at its own discretion, this creates an in-text contradiction because Article 4.2 and 4.3 still refer to 'Service Fee' without it being defined. This introduces uncertainty regarding how the fee is to be calculated and adjusted, and what the fee actually refers to, contradicting the intent of providing clarity on financial obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 The Service Fee of the last quarter shall be paid prior to the seventh business day following the commencement of the next quarter. Such Service Fee shall be paid to the bank account designated by Party B in writing. If Party B intends to change its bank account, it shall send a written notice to Party A seven business days in advance.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Service Fee' and its calculation. The changed text removes this definition, while Article 4.2 still refers to 'Service Fee'. This creates a contradiction because the term 'Service Fee' is used without a clear definition, making it unclear how it should be calculated or adjusted, directly impacting the meaning and enforceability of Article 4.2. This aligns with the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Term of Cooperation Party A and Party B agree and confirm that the term of cooperation under this Agreement shall commence from the execution date hereof and end on the expiration date of the operation term of Party B ("Term of Cooperation").
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Term of Cooperation Party A and Party B agree and confirm that the term of cooperation under this Agreement shall commence from the execution date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the definition of the 'Term of Cooperation' in Section 5, it creates an in-text contradiction because Section 6 refers to the 'Term of Cooperation' without it being properly defined, generating ambiguity regarding the contract's duration. This ambiguity may impact the enforcement of obligations and rights defined in the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the ending date of the 'Term of Cooperation' that was present in the original text. Section 6.1 refers to the 'expiration of the Term of Cooperation'. By removing the definition of when the 'Term of Cooperation' ends, the changed text creates a direct conflict/contradiction with Section 6.1, making it unclear when the agreement can be terminated. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' because essential language was removed creating a contradiction with other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all intellectual property researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B. For the purpose of this Article 12.3, "Intellectual Property" means the patent, patent application right, trademark, service mark, logo, image, trade name, internet domain name, design right, copyright (including copyright of computer software) and moral rights, database right, right of semiconductor design drawing, utility model, proprietary technology and other intellectual property that are registered and unregistered including those that have applied for registration, as well as all other rights or protection methods with same or similar effect on a global scope.
perturbation.changed_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all intellectual property researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of "Intellectual Property" in Section 12.3, this creates an in-text contradiction because the contract refers to ownership of intellectual property without explicitly defining what constitutes intellectual property under the scope of the agreement. This lack of definition will lead to disputes on what intellectual properties are under Party B ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.3 The Parties agree that any and all intellectual property researched and developed, created and invented by the Parties (including their employees) in the course of performance of this Agreement shall be owned by Party B.
perturbation.justification: The removal of the definition of 'Intellectual Property' in Section 12.3 creates a strong in-text contradiction. The changed text states that Party B owns all intellectual property developed during the agreement, but fails to define what constitutes 'Intellectual Property.' This directly contradicts the intent of the original text and the contradicted text itself (which is identical to the original in this case), as the agreement now lacks a clear scope for intellectual property ownership, leading to potential disputes and unenforceability. The omitted definition is essential for interpreting and enforcing the ownership clause. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.
